description: Canonical adoption-layer model and risk-tier mapping for the cursor-guardrails hub. Single source of truth for /guardrail-upgrade, the thin consumer starter (cursor-guardrails-starter), and downstream governance tools such as Throughline. Do not edit by hand — changes must be deliberate and version-bumped. Verified against the actual file tree by .github/scripts/check-manifest-drift.mjs in CI.

1.name: AI instructions
1.description: Rule files, slash commands, hooks, and cross-tool agent summary. Always safe to add — nothing here touches application code.

2.name: Git hygiene
2.description: Version control conventions, ignore rules, editor config, dependency management, legal, and contribution docs. Safe to add.

3.name: Commit discipline
3.description: Conventional Commits enforcement, pre-commit hooks, and lint-staged. Safe to add without touching application code.

4.name: Code quality toolchain
4.description: ESLint, Prettier, strict TypeScript. Merge carefully — may surface existing errors in application code.

5.name: CI pipeline
5.description: GitHub Actions: typecheck, lint, build, npm audit, secret scan (gitleaks), SAST (Semgrep). Merge carefully.

6.name: Code compliance audit
6.description: Manual audit of existing source code against installed rules. Classifies findings as Blocker / Should-fix / Nit. Not a file copy — a process step.

7.name: Governance activation
7.description: Human checklist: branch protection on main, Dependabot PRs reviewed, gh CLI installed. Not a file copy — a verification step.

Low.rationale: Low-risk initiatives require AI governance, version control discipline, and commit standards. Code quality and CI are strongly encouraged but not mandatory gates.

Medium.rationale: Medium-risk initiatives require full toolchain enforcement and a CI pipeline with security scanning in addition to the Low requirements.

High.rationale: High-risk initiatives require all of Medium plus a formal code compliance audit before build begins. Layer 7 governance activation is expected but recorded separately as a human checklist.

projectProfiles.description: Drives the Layer 0.4 'Project profile' step in guardrail-upgrade.md so the recommended layer set adapts to each project instead of a blanket 'apply all'. The risk question reuses riskTiers above — this block does not duplicate that mapping. Not every project has the same needs: a library needs different active domains than a full-stack app, and a low-risk prototype needs fewer required layers than a high-risk production service.

questions.prompt: What kind of project is this?
What is the risk level of this project?
Does the project already have its own ESLint/Prettier/tsconfig setup?
questions.effect: If yes, treat Layer 4 as extra merge-carefully: read both configs in full before adding strict settings, and never overwrite.

lifecycleStages.description: The Build-track (runs once per project) and Maintain-track (runs forever) stages documented in docs/project-lifecycle.md, encoded here as data so a downstream tool (e.g. Throughline) can detect which stage a project is in from deterministic GitHub facts — check-run results, PR review state, merge status — never from an AI's read of the code. ciCheckNames must match the exact GitHub check-run names this template's own CI produces (see .github/workflows/ci.yml); a project using different job names must adapt its own copy, this block is not auto-derived from ci.yml.

stages.name: Discover & Define
detectionSignals: Initiative recorded in the governance tool
First commit observed on the repository

stages.name: Design
detectionSignals: A file matching .cursor/plans/*.md present in a commit

stages.name: Build
detectionSignals: Commits present on a non-default branch
No check run from ciCheckNames.verify has concluded success yet

stages.name: Verify
detectionSignals: Latest check run named ciCheckNames.verify on the branch concluded success

stages.name: Secure
detectionSignals: Check runs named ciCheckNames.secretScan and ciCheckNames.sast both concluded success on the same commit as ciCheckNames.verify

stages.name: Review
detectionSignals: An open pull request exists with at least one review in the APPROVED state

stages.name: Release
detectionSignals: The pull request was merged to the default branch
All three ciCheckNames checks concluded success on the merge commit

stages.name: Operate & Maintain
detectionSignals: Ongoing after stage 7: .cursor/guardrail-version compared against the latest published guardrailVersion

templateMeta.description: Hub-only paths that live in this cursor-guardrails repo (playbook, Throughline contracts, scheduled AI reviews, version propagation) but must NEVER be copied into a consumer project by /guardrail-upgrade or synced into cursor-guardrails-starter. New products use the starter via GitHub 'Use this template'; this hub is the reference clone for upgrades. Worked example: docs/worked-example-africa-risk-map.md (PR that deleted ~5.5k lines of inherited hub meta).

schemaChangelog.note: Initial publication. Encodes 7 adoption layers and Low/Medium/High tier mapping. Introduces 10 rule files including four new holistic domains (31-design, 32-ux-behavioural, 33-data-science, 50-ai-tooling).

Adds .cursor/mcp.json and .cursorignore to Layer 1 — both already existed in the template and were already listed in guardrail-upgrade.md, but had drifted out of this manifest. Adds the check-manifest-drift.mjs CI guard so Layer 1 cannot silently go out of date again.

Adds three new rule files to Layer 1 — 60-backend-api.mdc, 61-database.mdc, 62-deployment-observability.mdc — and three new domains (Backend, Database, Deployment), closing the gap where the template had no guidance once a project adds a server, database, or deployment pipeline. guardrailVersion patch bump per the new-rule-file convention.

Adds .cursor/commands/audit.md to Layer 1 — a new /audit command for whole-repo, point-in-time health audits (distinct from the diff-scoped /review and template-comparison /guardrail-upgrade). guardrailVersion patch bump per the new-file-in-Layer-1 convention established for rule files, extended here to command files.

Adds a new top-level projectProfiles block — the data model for the Layer 0.4 'Project profile' step in guardrail-upgrade.md. Maps project type to active vs inert-until-used domains and reuses the existing riskTiers block for the layer recommendation, so adoption is tailored per project instead of a blanket 'apply all'. Additive schema change (schemaVersion 2 -> 3); guardrailVersion patch bump (the system improved).

No schema change — documents a /guardrail-upgrade behaviour improvement. Layer 0 (Bootstrap) now refreshes the local template clone with 'git pull --ff-only origin main' before reading any files from it, and falls back to a guardrail-version staleness warning if the pull cannot complete. Closes the gap where TEMPLATE_PATH could silently point at a months-old clone even though the published template had moved on. guardrailVersion patch bump (the system improved).

No schema change — documents two /guardrail-upgrade behaviour improvements. (1) Layer 0.4 (Project profile) now checks for a guardrail-prescription.json handoff file before asking its own 3 questions, so a Throughline (or other governance tool) classification carries through to execution instead of being re-answered and potentially diverging. See docs/guardrail-prescription.md for the contract. (2) 'Template clone' terminology is standardised to 'reference clone' across docs/playbook/README, and docs/connect-guardrails.md and docs/project-lifecycle.md are added as the canonical direct-vs-Throughline guide and the two-track SDLC lifecycle model respectively. guardrailVersion patch bump (the system improved).

Adds a new top-level lifecycleStages block — the data model for automated, deterministic detection of which of the 8 docs/project-lifecycle.md stages a project is in, derived from GitHub facts (check-run results, PR review state, merge status) rather than an AI's read of the code. Includes ciCheckNames so downstream tools (e.g. a Throughline GitHub App integration) key off this template's actual CI check-run names instead of guessing or hardcoding a second copy. Additive schema change (schemaVersion 3 -> 4); guardrailVersion minor bump (new mapping/domain added, per the existing convention).

Adds templateMeta — hub-only paths that must never be copied into consumer projects or the thin cursor-guardrails-starter. Driven by the Africa Risk Map worked example (docs/worked-example-africa-risk-map.md): 'Use this template' from the fat hub forced a ~5.5k-line day-one delete of playbook/Throughline/AI-review scaffolding. Layers 1–5 remain the only upgrade/sync surface. Also documents starterSource. Additive schema change (schemaVersion 4 -> 5); guardrailVersion minor bump 1.4.0 -> 1.5.0.

No schema change — Node/jsdom/Rolldown runtime guardrails. .nvmrc major 22 + package.json engines.node >=22.12.0 (Vite 8 / Rolldown floor; rejects the 22.11 engine gap). Pins jsdom to ^26.1.0 (ERR_REQUIRE_ESM / @exodus/bytes failure class). Adds .github/scripts/check-runtime.mjs (npm run check:runtime) wired in CI before test; syncs via starter EXTRA. guardrailVersion patch 1.5.0 -> 1.5.1.
